coredata.dc:title: Efficient tuning of nitrogen-doped carbon dots phosphorescence based on substrate regulation for multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Qingxuan
Gan, Sanpeng
Cheng, Qian
Jiang, Zhao
Zhu, Haifeng
Xie, Gongxing
Liu, Rui
Zhu, Senqiang
Zhu, Hongjun
coredata.dc:description:
The development of an accurate phosphorescent regulation strategy is key to customizing carbon dots (CDs) for anti-counterfeiting applications. To this target, we design a simple substrate regulation technique to manipulate phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCD). In this work, a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach is proposed for combining NCD with urea (UA), boric acid (BA) and silica (SiO2) through hydrogen bonding or covalent bonding, a series of RTP composites were designed and synthesized. The RTP wavelength and lifetime of the composites can be tuned effectively, by preparing composite materials (NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 ) composed of NCD and different substrate (UA; BA; SiO2). The average RTP lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 are 332.26 ms, 652.05 ms and 1587.22 ms, respectively. Furthermore, the RTP mechanism is verified. This facilitates the application of RTP materials and the adjustment of properties to practical requirements. Owing to the different phosphorescent wavelength and lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , time-dependent multiple anti-counterfeiting can be achieved. Cell experiments show that NCD, NCD/BA, NCD/UA and NCD/SiO
2 have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. This result indicates these RTP materials can be potentially used to realize multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Substrate regulation
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Long lifetime
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCD, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
Characterization of NCD, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
Photophysical properties of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
RTP mechanism of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20170104 ), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds ( 2021K136B ), “Six Talent Peaks Project” of Jiangsu Province ( XCL-037 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFB0307202 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of NCD. (b) Size distribution of NCD. (c) XRD pattern of NCD. (d) FTIR spectrum of NCD. (e) XPS spectrum of NCD. (f–h) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum, N 1s XPS spectrum and O 1s XPS spectrum of NCD.

(a–c) FTIR spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , (e) B 1s spectrum of NCD/BA, (f) Si 2p spectrum of NCD/SiO
2 .

(a–c) Fluorescence emission spectra and phosphorescence emission spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , excited at 373 nm, 273 nm and 290 nm respectively. (d–f) Afterglow decay curves of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , excited at 373 nm, 273 nm, 290 nm and monitored at their optimal wavelengths respectively.

(a–c) ESR spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , (d) RTP mechanism of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of NCD and NCD composites.

head.ce:title: Efficient tuning of nitrogen-doped carbon dots phosphorescence based on substrate regulation for multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mingyue Optical Lens Co., Ltd, Zhenjiang, 212300, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mingyue Optical Lens Co., Ltd. Zhenjiang, 212300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The development of an accurate phosphorescent regulation strategy is key to customizing carbon dots (CDs) for anti-counterfeiting applications. To this target, we design a simple substrate regulation technique to manipulate phosphorescence of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCD). In this work, a cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach is proposed for combining NCD with urea (UA), boric acid (BA) and silica (SiO2) through hydrogen bonding or covalent bonding, a series of RTP composites were designed and synthesized. The RTP wavelength and lifetime of the composites can be tuned effectively, by preparing composite materials (NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 ) composed of NCD and different substrate (UA; BA; SiO2). The average RTP lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 are 332.26 ms, 652.05 ms and 1587.22 ms, respectively. Furthermore, the RTP mechanism is verified. This facilitates the application of RTP materials and the adjustment of properties to practical requirements. Owing to the different phosphorescent wavelength and lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , time-dependent multiple anti-counterfeiting can be achieved. Cell experiments show that NCD, NCD/BA, NCD/UA and NCD/SiO
2 have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. This result indicates these RTP materials can be potentially used to realize multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decades, a variety of anti-counterfeiting technologies have been developed, including the recent magnetic response [1,2], plasmonic security labels [3–5] and luminescence printing [6–8]. Among these, luminescence printing offers advantages in easy handling, high-throughput, and facile design. The traditional luminescent materials used in anti-counterfeiting generally exhibit unicolor or multicolor luminescence upon excitation by ultraviolet (UV) light. However, similar emission characteristics can be obtained by utilizing certain substitutes or simply mixing different fluorescent materials, thus exhibiting poor anti-counterfeiting performance. The clonable decryption of conventional luminescence printing largely restricts their application in high-security-level anti-counterfeiting. Therefore, room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are considered as an advanced anti-counterfeiting material because they can show different emission properties when the excitation UV light is turned on and off. RTP materials were highlighted the potential application in multi-level lifetime-tuned anti-counterfeiting, which can compensate for the shortcomings of traditional anti-counterfeiting methods.

However, it is difficult to emit efficient RTP signals due to the propensity for the energy of the excited triplet states to dissipate through thermal vibrations, collisions, or exposure to quenchers like oxygen. Conventional organic RTP materials are produced by forming specific structures (i.e., clusters, host-guest compositions, and polymer substrates) [9–18] to stabilize the triple excited state, or by introducing special groups (i.e., heavy halogens and aromatic aldehydes) [19,20] to enhance the intersystem crossing (ISC). However, most of the organic RTP materials have short afterglow lifetime, low photothermal stability and high toxicity. Moreover, the phosphorescent emission of organic RTP materials is easily quenched by oxygen/steam, which greatly limits its potential applications [21,22].

In recent years, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their advantages of simple preparation, high photostability and low toxicity [23,24]. In 2013, Zhao et al. reported the first example of RTP material based on CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which shows a long lifetime of ∼380 ms [25]. The carbonyl group on the surface of CDs promotes ISC, and the formation of hydrogen bond between PVA and CDs inhibits non-radiative transition. This strategy can reduce non-radiative transitions by inhibiting intermolecular vibrations, thus enabling the CDs to exhibit obvious RTP properties. To date, various matrices, including inorganic complexes [26–29] and polymers/organic compounds [13,30–32] have been adopted to fabricate phosphorescent materials based on CDs. However, the single phosphorescence lifetime and color of the previously reported CDs were uninsured to achieve anti-counterfeiting missions in the current high-tech community. Therefore, developing a precise and reproducible phosphorescence tuning approach is urgently needed.

In this work, we employed the one-pot microwave route for the preparation of N-doped carbon dots (NCD) without strong acids, bases or further surface passivation. All the raw materials are economical and non-toxic, and the preparation method is environment-friendly and efficient. The introduction of nitrogen is beneficial to the formation of CN, which can promote the ISC efficiently. As solid matrixes, the Urea (UA), boric acid (BA) and silica gel (SiO2) can reduce the effect of oxygen and water on phosphor quenching (Scheme 1 ). These matrices (UA; BA; SiO2) can effectively fix emissive species and prevent the NCD triplet states from quenching, and the synthesis process of these matrices is relatively simple. In particular, the RTP of the produced NCD can be precisely tuned by engineering the substrates, demonstrating the high designability of the phosphorescence tuning of our substrate regulation technique. More importantly, the lifetime of NCD based RTP materials are much longer than that of traditional organic RTP materials. Therefore, these obtained RTP materials show great potentials in multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting.

All chemicals and reagents were obtained from commercial sources without further purification. Solvents for chemical synthesis were purified according to standard procedure. The instruments and models used in this article are provided in the ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCD, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of NCD. 3 g of citric acid and 6 g of urea were added to 20 mL deionized water to form a transparent solution. The mixed solution was microwaved for 5 min in a 650 W microwave oven for deep heating. Finally, the NCD solution was obtained. The products were centrifuged and dialyzed for purification and freeze-dried for further use [33].

Synthesis of NCD/UA. NCD (2 mg) and urea (6 g) were mixed respectively in 10 mL deionized water to obtain NCD/UA solutions in beakers by shaking for 10 min to completely dissolve the urea. Then the beakers were put into oven at 160 °C for 6 h and then cooled down to room temperature to get NCD/UA [32].

Synthesis of NCD/BA. 3 g BA were firstly diluted with 40 mL deionized water and mixed with 2 mg NCD in a beaker. Then the beaker was covered with foil to prevent the water from evaporating too fast. Afterwards, the beaker was put in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h and cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final products were ground into powder [34].

Synthesis of NCD/SiO
2 . NCD/SiO
2 was prepared by mixing tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ethanol, H2O, and HCl (pH = 2) in a molar ratio of 1:4:5:0.2 followed by refluxing at 80 °C for 120 min. A dispersion containing NCD (2 mg) was then added into the above solution (20 mL) and kept under stirring at room temperature until a uniform gel formed. The dried gel powders were then calcined at 600 °C for 90 min to obtain the final NCD/SiO
2 nanocomposite [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCD, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the NCD was studied through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The NCD particles have good dispersion and quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 2.8 nm, and a lattice fringe (Fig. 1 a–b). Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCD also presents an amorphous state (Fig. 1c). A broad peak at 2θ of 26.8° in the XRD pattern is due to the highly disordered carbon atoms. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of NCD (Fig. 1d) displays characteristic absorption bands at 1602 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 3421 cm−1, which are attributed to CN, CO and –OH/N–H stretching vibrations, respectively.

To further understand the composition of NCD, its X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained. The results of XPS (Fig. 1e) show that NCD are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1f) shows three peaks at 284.6, 285.9, and 288.0 eV for sp2 C, C–N and CN/CO respectively [32]. N 1s bands (Fig. 1g) reveal the presence of N atoms mainly in the form of pyridine N (399.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.8 eV), and graphite N (400.6 eV). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) shows two peaks at 531.5 and 533.2 eV that can be assigned to CO and C–OH bonds [33].

To deduce the chemical interactions of NCD composites, FTIR and XPS of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, FTIR spectra display characteristic absorption bands at 3217–3481 cm−1, which are attributed to –OH/N–H stretching vibrations and hydrogen bonded [14,26,29,32]. It is worth noting that FTIR spectrum of NCD/BA (Fig. 2b) displays characteristic absorption band at 1466 cm−1, which are attributed to B–O stretching vibrations. The band peaked at 1189 cm−1 is originated from an asymmetrically stretched oxygen atom connecting the trigonal boron atoms. The new band at 948 cm−1 is attributed to the characteristic boron–carbon bond [34]. Meanwhile, in Fig. 2c, FTIR spectrum of NCD/SiO
2 displays characteristic absorption bands at 800 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1, which are put down to C–Si and Si–O–C stretching vibrations. Compared with NCD/UA, NCD can be homogeneously dispersed in BA and SiO2 in the form of covalent bonds (Figs. S1–S2 and Tables S1–S2). These results are consistent with XPS analyses (Fig. 2d–e and Figs. S3–S5). In Fig. 2d, the B 1s spectrum of NCD/BA can be fitted to three bands centered at 194.4 eV, 193.4 eV, and 192.4 eV, which belong to C–B, B2O3 and B–O [34]. The Si 2p spectrum of NCD/SiO
2 (Fig. 2e) can be fitted to three bands centered at 103.6 eV, 104.9 eV and 105.3 eV, which are attributed to C–Si, SiO2 and Si–O, respectively [35]. As can be seen from FTIR and XPS spectra, NCD is mainly dispersed in UA through hydrogen bonding. Meanwhile, when NCD is dispersed in BA and SiO2, covalent bonding and hydrogen bonding work together. These results also lay the foundation for tuning the phosphorescent wavelength and lifetimes of NCD by substrates regulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of NCD were systemically studied via ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). Fig. S6 displays the absorption spectra of NCD. The UV–Vis spectra of NCD in aqueous solution show strong absorption peaks at ∼200 nm and a shoulder peak at around 320 nm. These peaks are attributed to the π−π* transition of aromatic (CC and C–C) bonds from the carbon core and the n−π* transition (CO and C–N) bonds in conjugated sp2 domains. Moreover, in wavelength ranges longer than 420 nm, the NCD show characteristic peak. Those peaks in this regime can commonly be assigned to the surface state transition (CO, CN, C–O) of the shell of NCD [23]. The UV absorption spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA, NCD/SiO
2 also show similar characteristic absorption, which indicates the successful loading of their NCD. Fig. S7 presents the PL excitation spectra of the NCD in aqueous solutions, the maximum emission at 510 nm. The PL excitation spectrum highly overlapping with the absorption band of CO bonds, suggesting that the luminescence should come from the CO bonds of NCD. The CIE coordinates of NCD are calculated by its emission spectra. The results show that NCD/UA, NCD/BA, NCD/SiO
2 , exhibits different emission colors, mainly due to the quenching triplet of the NCD in aqueous solution.

The photophysical properties of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 were fully investigated. Fig. 3 a–c displays that the fluorescence (FL) and RTP emission spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 . Their RTP emission peaks appear at 453 nm, 501 nm and 483 nm, respectively. This indicates the phosphorescent wavelength of NCD can be controlled by substrate regulation. Combined with fluorescent emission spectra of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , the ΔEST was calculated and listed in Table S3, all of which are small values (0.05–0.44 eV) for effective ISC process and spin–orbit coupling. Based on phosphorescent emission mechanism, the energy level of S1 is always higher than that of T1, so NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 with long wavelength FL emission are favorable for acquiring longer wavelength RTP. Compared with RTP spectrum of NCD/UA, NCD/BA, NCD/SiO
2 , FL spectrum of these matrices showed blue shift. These results are amply justified that the new emerged covalent bond C–B and C–Si can regulate singlet and triplet energy levels of the NCD.

In order to further study the effect of different substrates on phosphorescent properties, the RTP lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 were also measured. According to the following equation (1), an average lifetime was thus calculated [34,35]. As displayed in Fig. 3d–f and Tables S4–S6, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 exhibited the longest phosphorescent lifetime when 2 mg of NCD was added. The average RTP lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 was calculated to be 332.26 ms, 652.05 ms and 1587.22 ms respectively. We calculated the CIE coordinates, and the results show that it has a good color difference ability, and the different lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA, NCD/SiO
2 , it shows property of multi-level lifetime-tuned anti-counterfeiting (Fig. S8). Compared with NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 showed longer lifetime due to the covalent bond between the NCD and BA or SiO2. Therefore, compared to what were reported for CD-based RTP materials previously, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 exhibit much longer RTP lifetime (Table S7).
(1)
τ
ave = ∑
ɑ
i
τ
i
2
/
∑
ɑ
i
τ
i

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTP mechanism of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
The source of phosphorescence is assumed to come from the NCD. Firstly, the CN and CO at the surface of the NCD can improve coupling degree of spin orbit to enhance ISC and produce stable triple excited states, thus leading to phosphorescent emission [36,37]. To verify our hypothesis, electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR) spectra of the samples were tested to capture the stable triple excited states. As shown in Fig. 4 a–c, NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 exhibit the distinct ESR signal at 3490–3510 G. However, individual NCD, UA, BA, SiO2 do not exhibit ESR signals (Fig. S9). This indicated that the stable triple excited state can be generate by NCD after recombination with UA, BA and SiO2 [35,38]. Secondly, hydrogen bonds formed between NCD and the matrix rigidify CN and CO bonds of NCD to reduce non-radiative transitions (Fig. 4d) [32,39,40]. Compared with NCD/UA, the C–Si (NCD/SiO
2 ) can further restrict vibration and rotation to reduce non-radiative transitions. Thus, compared with NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 have a longer RTP lifetime and blue shift in phosphorescent emission. Finally, UA, BA and SiO2 as solid matrices can reduce triple excited state quenching by water and oxygen.

Owing to the different phosphorescent wavelength and lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 , their potential applications in multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting technology were demonstrated. By dispersing NCD in starch, we obtained NCD/starch powder (the details are provided in the ESI). By adopting NCD/UA, NCD/BA, NCD/SiO
2 and NCD/starch, an anti-counterfeiting application model has been designed. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the numbers "2″ and "3″ were printed by screen printing under 254 nm UV lamp, in which "2″ is printed by NCD/starch as ink, whereas "3″ is printed by NCD/SiO
2 . Due to the phosphorescence of NCD/SiO
2 at room temperature, the number "3″ can still be seen after turning off the 254 nm UV lamp. Next, by using NCD/UA, a flower pattern is obtained by screen printing. Under 254 or 365 nm UV lamps, the flower pattern is pale blue. Because NCD/UA has RTP properties, the pattern of a flower can still be seen after turning off the 254 nm UV lamp. However, compared with the pattern when the 254 nm UV lamp is turned off, the color of the flower changes from blue to green when the 365 nm UV lamp is turned off, so multi-color anti-counterfeiting can be achieved (Fig. 5b). In Fig. 5c, NCD/UA and NCD/BA were used as inks to obtain two numbers "8″ by screen printing. The left number "8″ was printed by using the ink of NCD/UA, and the other "8″ was printed by NCD/BA. Since NCD/UA and NCD/BA both have RTP properties, two numbers "8″ can still be seen after turning off the 254 nm UV light. However, after turning off 254 nm UV lamp for 1 s, the left number "8″ gradually dimmed. When the UV lamp is turned off for 2 s, due to the different phosphorescence lifetimes of NCD/UA and NCD/BA, the left number "8″ disappeared, and only the right number "8″ can be seen. Therefore, time-dependent multiple anti-counterfeiting can be achieved. In addition, cell viability tests were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NCD, NCD/BA, NCD/UA and NCD/SiO
2 to L929 cell (Fig. S10). The results show that NCD, NCD/BA, NCD/UA and NCD/SiO
2 have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Based on these results, these RTP materials can potentially realize multiple anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, a green and facile technique using a one-pot microwave route for the preparation of N-doped carbon dots (NCD) was presented. CN/CO bonds on the surface of NCD can facilitate ISC and generate triple excited state, which is believed to be the origin of phosphorescence. By combining NCD with UA, BA and SiO2 through hydrogen bonding or covalent bonding, a series of RTP composites were designed and synthesized. The RTP wavelength and lifetime of the composites can be tuned effectively by the interaction between NCD and substrates. The average RTP lifetime of NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 were calculated to be 332.26 ms, 652.05 ms and 1587.22 ms respectively. NCD/UA, NCD/BA and NCD/SiO
2 powders show great potential in multicolor and time-dependent anti-counterfeiting. These applications expand the practical application of organic RTP materials. These results are of significance to the rational design of organic RTP materials.

Qingxuan Meng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Sanpeng Gan: Writing – review & editing. Qian Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Zhao Jiang: Conceptualization. Haifeng Zhu: Data curation. Gongxing Xie: Data curation. Rui Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Senqiang Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongjun Zhu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20170104), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (2021K136B), “Six Talent Peaks Project” of Jiangsu Province (XCL-037), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0307202).

J Mater Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new anti‐counterfeiting feature relying on invisible luminescent full color images printed with lanthanide‐based inks

Nature Mater

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-afterglow metal–organic frameworks: reversible guest-induced phosphorescence tunability
